perturbation.original_text: 9. INDEMNITY: Without limiting any other agreement contained in this Agreement, CLIENT agrees to defend, indemnify and hold harmless BOSCH and its parents, subsidiary and affiliated entities, successors, assigns and licensees, and their respective officers, agents, directors, owners, shareholders and employees, harmless from any and all claims, actions or proceedings of any kind and from any and all damages, liabilities, costs and expenses (including reasonable legal fees) relating to or arising out of any claim by a third Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. INDEMNITY: Without limiting any other agreement contained in this Agreement, CLIENT agrees to defend, indemnify and hold harmless BOSCH and its parents, subsidiary and affiliated entities, successors, assigns and licensees, and their respective officers, agents, directors, owners, shareholders and employees, harmless from any and all claims, actions or proceedings of any kind and from any and all damages, liabilities, costs and expenses (including legal fees up to $5000) relating to or arising out of any claim by a third Party.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting legal fees to $5000 in an indemnity clause may violate state laws requiring full indemnification for actual losses, including legal expenses. This is especially true if the actual legal fees exceed $5000, potentially leaving Bosch under-indemnified.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 681A.020 broadly defines insurance and indemnity, and limiting legal fees could be construed as an insufficient attempt to indemnify the harmed party, potentially violating the spirit and intent of indemnity laws requiring complete coverage of losses.

NRS 681A.1556 Certified reinsurer: Applicant certified in certain jurisdictions may be deemed by Commissioner to be certified in Nevada. NRS 681A.1557 Certified rei
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a cap of $5000 on legal fees within the indemnity clause, potentially contradicting the principle of full indemnification for losses. While the provided Nevada statute NRS 681A.020, mentioned in the Law Explanation, isn't directly quoted, the explanation suggests that limiting legal fees could be seen as an insufficient attempt to indemnify the harmed party, thus violating the spirit of indemnity laws requiring complete coverage of losses. This directly contradicts the original intent of indemnity, which is to protect the indemnitee fully from any losses or damages that occur.

perturbation.original_text: (v) ASSISTANCE TO CLIENT: In addition to its other obligations under this Agreement, BOSCH shall assist CLIENT in obtaining (and provide all documentation required to obtain) such licenses and/or permits as may be necessary or desirable for the sales and/or Distribution and/or Placement of the Product for city, state and/or federal contract accounts (Exit signs in and on buildings, mandatory safety fixtures, freeway signs, etc...) within the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: (v) ASSISTANCE TO CLIENT: BOSCH may, at its sole discretion, assist CLIENT in obtaining such licenses and/or permits as may be necessary or desirable for the sales and/or Distribution and/or Placement of the Product for city, state and/or federal contract accounts (Exit signs in and on buildings, mandatory safety fixtures, freeway signs, etc...) within the Territory. BOSCH is not responsible for any delays in obtaining such licenses.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall assist' to 'may, at its sole discretion, assist' weakens Bosch's obligation to help Client obtain necessary permits. For certain regulated products (e.g., safety equipment), this could conflict with implied duties of good faith and fair dealing in contract law, if Bosch's lack of assistance prevents the Client from legally selling the product.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. If the Client cannot reasonably sell the product without these licenses, Bosch's discretionary assistance could be seen as a breach of good faith, especially if Bosch is aware that the Client depends on their help.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall assist' to 'may, at its sole discretion, assist' creates a legal contradiction. The original text imposed a clear obligation on BOSCH to assist the CLIENT in obtaining necessary licenses. The changed text weakens this obligation, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith as explained in the Law Explanation and supported by UCC § 1-304, which states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement'. The change introduces a level of discretion that could allow BOSCH to avoid assisting the CLIENT, which, if that assistance is critical for the CLIENT to legally sell the product, would violate the obligation of good faith. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this is particularly relevant for regulated products.

perturbation.original_text: 10. CONFIDENTIALITY: CLIENT acknowledges that irreparable injury and damage will result from the disclosure to any third party of Proprietary Information associated with the Product and agrees (1) not to disclose any Proprietary Information to any third party, and (2) not to allow any third party to possess, handle, disassemble, touch, photograph, film or otherwise record images of the Product while the Product is upon CLIENT'S premises or in CLIENT'S possession, custody or control if the product is or has not been purchased with a purchase order and payment fulfilled.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. CONFIDENTIALITY: CLIENT acknowledges that irreparable injury and damage will result from the disclosure to any third party of Proprietary Information associated with the Product and agrees (1) not to disclose any Proprietary Information to any third party, unless compelled by a subpoena, and (2) not to allow any third party to possess, handle, disassemble, touch, photograph, film or otherwise record images of the Product while the Product is upon CLIENT'S premises or in CLIENT'S possession, custody or control if the product is or has not been purchased with a purchase order and payment fulfilled.
perturbation.explanation: While it is acceptable to have a clause that says you are not required to keep information confidential if compelled by subpoena. By not specifying the required procedure (written notice to the other party allowing them time to respond to the subpoena) prior to releasing Confidential Information when compelled by a subpoena, this violates laws designed to prevent the release of trade secrets and/or confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA Section 5 allows for injunctive relief to prevent the disclosure of trade secrets. By not specifying the requirement of written notice and reasonable time to respond prior to releasing confidential information compelled by subpoena the contract facilitates a potential violation of this law because it allows the recipient of the subpoena to immediately disclose information that could qualify as a trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces an exception to the confidentiality obligation (disclosure compelled by subpoena) without specifying a required procedure to notify the other party, potentially allowing for immediate disclosure of confidential information. According to the Law Explanation, UTSA Section 5 allows for injunctive relief to prevent the disclosure of trade secrets. By not requiring written notice and reasonable time to respond, the changed contract language facilitates a potential violation of this law, creating a legal contradiction because it undermines the ability to prevent disclosure of trade secrets through injunctive relief. The changed text allows for immediate disclosure, which is inconsistent with preventing disclosure via injunctive relief.
